phases.goal: Know exactly where your money goes and set a clear target

steps.title: Calculate your net worth
steps.description: List everything you own (assets: savings, investments, property) and everything you owe (liabilities: loans, credit cards, mortgage). Net worth = assets minus liabilities. This is your financial baseline — you can't improve what you don't measure.
steps.success_metric: Net worth calculated and written down

steps.title: Track every expense for 30 days
steps.description: Use a budgeting app or spreadsheet to categorise every purchase for a full month. Don't change your behaviour yet — just observe. Most people underestimate their spending in 2–3 categories significantly.
steps.success_metric: 30 consecutive days of expense tracking completed

steps.title: Set a specific financial target
steps.description: Define a concrete goal: 'Save £10,000 emergency fund by December', 'Pay off £5,000 credit card debt in 12 months', or 'Invest £500/month for 2 years'. Vague goals produce vague results.
steps.success_metric: Specific financial target defined with a deadline

phases.goal: Eliminate money leaks, pay down debt, and automate savings

steps.title: Build a zero-based budget
steps.description: Give every pound/dollar a job. Allocate income across essentials, savings, debt repayment, and discretionary spending until income minus all allocations equals zero. Automate savings on payday before you can spend them.
steps.success_metric: Zero-based budget created and first month tracked against it

steps.title: Build a 3-month emergency fund
steps.description: Before investing, build a cash buffer of 3 months of essential expenses. Keep it in a high-yield savings account. This prevents you from raiding investments in a crisis and eliminates the anxiety of living with no margin.
steps.success_metric: Emergency fund target set and automatic contributions running

steps.title: Attack high-interest debt
steps.description: List all debts by interest rate. Minimum payments on all. Every extra pound goes to the highest-rate debt first (avalanche method) — this is mathematically optimal. Alternatively, pay off smallest balance first for a psychological win (snowball method).
steps.success_metric: Debt repayment plan running with first debt targeted

phases.goal: Start investing consistently and build long-term wealth habits

steps.title: Max employer pension/401k match
steps.description: If your employer matches pension or 401k contributions, maximise this before anything else. Matching is an instant 50–100% return on your money — no investment can beat it. Contribute at least enough to get the full match.

steps.title: Open an investment account and start investing
steps.description: Open a low-cost index fund account (ISA, Roth IRA, or equivalent). Start with a broad global index fund. Set up a monthly standing order. Investing £200/month at 8% average return over 20 years grows to over £110,000.
steps.success_metric: Investment account open with recurring monthly contribution set up

steps.title: Automate and review quarterly
steps.description: Set your budget, savings, and investments to run automatically. Review quarterly — not monthly — to avoid emotional decision-making from short-term fluctuations. The best investors are the ones who forget they have accounts.
steps.success_metric: All financial systems automated and first quarterly review scheduled
